checks.description: Google service account JSON key file committed to repo
checks.fix: Revoke the key immediately in Google Cloud Console > IAM > Service Accounts. Remove from git history with: git filter-repo --invert-paths --path <file> Use Workload Identity Federation or Secret Manager instead of key files.
checks.reason: A Google service account JSON key committed to the repo gives anyone (including AI agents scanning files) full API access to whatever IAM roles that service account holds — often project-wide.

checks.description: GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS points to a file path (key file used)
checks.fix: In GCP: use Workload Identity (no key file needed). In CI: inject credentials via Secret Manager or GitHub OIDC, not file path. Verify the key file is not committed: git ls-files | grep -i credentials
checks.reason: Setting GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS to a file path means a service account key JSON exists on disk and the agent can read it via the env var. This is the pattern google-auth-library flags as high risk for container/CI envs.

checks.description: OAuth2 client secret exposed in source
checks.fix: Rotate the client secret in Google Cloud Console > APIs & Services > Credentials. Load via environment variable: process.env.GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET
checks.reason: Google OAuth2 client secrets exposed in source allow anyone to impersonate your application and initiate OAuth flows on its behalf.

checks.description: Overly broad Google OAuth scope requested
checks.fix: Replace with the narrowest scope for the operation: cloud-platform → storage.readonly or bigquery.readonly admin.directory → admin.directory.users.readonly Audit all scopes in your OAuth consent screen.
checks.reason: Broad OAuth scopes like cloud-platform or admin.directory grant the agent access to resources far beyond what it needs. google-api-nodejs-client documentation emphasizes minimum-scope principle: request only the specific scopes each operation requires.

checks.description: Service account key file referenced in GitHub Actions workflow
checks.fix:
Migrate to Workload Identity Federation: uses: google-github-actions/auth@v2 with:
  workload_identity_provider: projects/123/locations/global/workloadIdentityPools/...
  service_account: my-sa@my-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com
checks.reason: Using google-github-actions/auth with credentials_json (a key file) instead of workload_identity_provider means a long-lived service account key is stored as a GitHub secret — rotatable but still a permanent credential.

checks.description: gcloud auth activate-service-account with key file in CI script
checks.fix: Use Workload Identity Federation or Application Default Credentials instead of explicit key file activation.
checks.reason: Activating a service account with a key file in CI means the key file must exist on disk, making it accessible to any process in that job.

checks.description: Firebase Admin SDK initialized with service account credential object inline
checks.fix: Use Application Default Credentials: admin.initializeApp({ credential: admin.credential.applicationDefault() }) or load from environment: admin.credential.cert(JSON.parse(process.env.FIREBASE_SA))
checks.reason: Inlining service account credentials in Firebase Admin initialization means the key data lives in source code and is scanned by AI agents.

checks.description: API key passed as query parameter in source code
checks.fix: Pass the key via Authorization header or x-goog-api-key header. Never embed API keys in URLs that appear in logs or get shared.
checks.reason: API keys in URL query strings appear in server logs, browser history, and Referer headers — making them effectively public. Google API documentation explicitly prohibits passing API keys as query params in production code.

checks.description: JWT signed with HS256 and hardcoded secret (symmetric key)
checks.fix: Use RS256 or ES256 with a proper key pair. Load the signing key from environment or secret manager, never hardcode.
checks.reason: HS256 JWTs with hardcoded symmetric secrets mean every service that verifies the token also knows the signing key — any one of them can forge tokens. google-auth-library uses asymmetric RS256/ES256 for this reason.

checks.description: credentials.json or token.json file present in tracked paths
checks.fix: Add to .gitignore: credentials.json, token.json, *-sa-key.json Verify none are tracked: git ls-files | grep -iE "(credentials|token|sa-key)\.json"
checks.reason: Files with these names are the default output of gcloud, Google OAuth client libraries, and service account key downloads. They should never be committed.

checks.description: TODO or FIXME referencing OAuth, token, or credentials
checks.fix: Resolve before merging. Do not defer credential security as a future task.
checks.reason: TODOs about credentials frequently indicate a temporary hardcoded value or a placeholder that was never properly replaced with a secure alternative.
